title: R N A-Seq with Human Skin Biopsies. As described elsewhere in the volume, consumption of grapes modulates gene expression in the skin of humans. This is confirmed by the R N A-seq data shown below, from a human subject referred to by the code letter S. Comparison of a skin biopsy obtained prior to the consumption of grapes for two weeks with a biopsy obtained following grape consumption for two weeks showed 21,507 gene transcripts shared in common, while 1894 genes were uniquely expressed in the pre-grape sample, and 2296 were uniquely expressed in the post-grape sample. Thus, grape consumption, per se, influences gene expression.

sentences.text: R N A-Seq with Human Skin Biopsies.
As described elsewhere in the volume, consumption of grapes modulates gene expression in the skin of humans.

This is confirmed by the R N A-seq data shown below, from a human subject referred to by the code letter S. Comparison of a skin biopsy obtained prior to the consumption of grapes for two weeks with a biopsy obtained following grape consumption for two weeks showed 21,507 gene transcripts shared in common, while 1894 genes were uniquely expressed in the pre-grape sample, and 2296 were uniquely expressed in the post-grape sample.
Thus, grape consumption, per se, influences gene expression.
Also, ultraviolet irradiation of human skin, per se, results in phenotypic alterations.
This is illustrated by the comparison of pre-grape no ultraviolet exposure and pre-grape with ultraviolet exposure.

In this analysis, there were 19,495 transcripts in common with both specimens, but 3906 transcripts were unique to the no ultraviolet sample, and 2480 transcripts were unique to the ultraviolet sample.
The next question to be addressed is how the expression pattern compares with exposure to ultraviolet, both before and after grape consumption.
This is illustrated by the comparison of no grape consumption with ultraviolet exposure and two weeks of grape consumption with ultraviolet exposure.

In this case, with ultraviolet-exposed specimens, 19,225 transcripts were shared between the two samples, whereas 2750 and 3443 unique transcripts were observed with the pre-grape and post-grape samples, respectively.
Finally, the results obtained with and without ultraviolet exposure were compared following two weeks of grape consumption.
This is illustrated with two weeks of grape consumption with no ultraviolet irradiation versus two weeks of grape consumption with ultraviolet irradiation.
The results showed 20,687 transcripts shared between the two samples, with 3116 and 1981 unique transcripts observed in the no ultraviolet and ultraviolet samples, respectively.
An overlay of all these data is provided in the Venn diagram that includes all four specimens.
As expected, based on the results described above, each of the four specimens showed unique expression patterns not in common with one another.
Relative to the side-by-side comparisons, however, the number of unique transcripts was reduced by roughly two- to threefold, to the range of 800 to 1200.
It is likely that some of these phenotypic changes induced by grape consumption have downstream consequences that affect responses to ultraviolet irradiation.
Numerically, the same patterns of gene expression were observed with three additional human subjects.
We are currently in the process of identifying differentially expressed genes and interpreting the biological relevance of the changes.
